coredata.dc:title: Facile preparation of N, S co-doped carbon dots and their application to a novel off-on fluorescent probe for selective determination of Hg2+
coredata.dc:creator: Yin, Pengyuan
Yao, Guixiang
Zou, Tianru
A, Nan
Na, Pei
Yang, Wenrong
Wang, Hongbin
Tan, Wei
coredata.dc:description:
In this work, citric acid (CA) and glutathione (GSH) were selected as the raw materials for preparation carbon dote co-doped with N and S (NS-CDs) via microwave heating method. The obtained NS-CDs displayed stable and strong blue luminescence with a maximum emission (λem) wavelength of 442 nm and a maximum excitation (λex) wavelength of 371 nm, and their fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) was 10.9%. The fluorescence of NS-CDs was quenched by silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) through a combination of static quenching effect and inner filter effect (IFE), and could be recovered by Hg2+ even at nanomolar levels ascribing to redox reaction between Hg2+ and AgNPs. Based upon this phenomenon, a novel off-on fluorescent probe (NS-CDs - AgNPs) for determination trace Hg2+ in aqueous solution was constructed using the obtained NS-CDs and AgNPs. Under optimal conditions, NS-CDs - AgNPs exhibited a wide linearity range of 10–90 nM (R
2 = 0.993) for Hg2+and a low LOD of 4.2 nM. NS-CDs - AgNPs was successfully utilized for monitoring Hg2+ in real water samples with the recoveries of 91.4–108.6% (RSD <5.84%), which indicated that the fluorescent probe has enormous application potential to detect trace Hg2+ in real water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent probe
Hg2+
Silver nanoparticle

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of NS-CDs (a) and AgNPs (b); (c) FT-IR of NS-CDs; (d) XPS survey spectrum of NS-CDs.

XPS high-resolution spectra spectrum of (a) C1S, (b) N1S, (c) O1S, (d) S2p. (e) UV–vis spectra, fluorescence excitation (Em) and emission spectra (Ex) of NS-CDs; the insert consists of the digital photo of the NS-CDs solution under sunlight (left) and UV lamp at 365 nm (right). (f) Fluorescence spectra of NS-CDs at different excitation wavelengths.

(a) The NS-CDs fluorescence value influence by AgNPs or/and Hg2+ (NS-CDs: 0.01 g/L, AgNPs:0.06 g/L, Hg2+: 50 nM). (b) UV–vis spectrum and (c) fluorescence decay curves of the NS-CDs, AgNPs, NS-CDs-AgNPs and NS-CDs- AgNPs-Hg2+ (NS-CDs: 0.01 g/L AgNPs:0.06 g/L, Hg2+: 50 nM). (d) Ultraviolet spectrum of NS-CDs -AgNPs after Hg2+ was added.

(a) Fluorescence response of NS-CDs – AgNPs after adding different concentration of Hg2+ (10–90 nM); the inserted image is of the linear relationship between ΔF and Hg2+ concentration. (b) Fluorescence response of NS-CDs – AgNPs for 50 nM Hg2+ to other mental ions at the concentration of 50 nM (pink bar) and with the coexisting other mental ions at the concentration of 250 nM (yellow bar).
Process of NS-CDs–AgNPs synthesis and Hg2+ detection.
Comparison of the developed method for Hg2+ detection with some reported methods.

row.entry: NS-CDs – AgNPs citric acid and glutathione

row.entry: Discharge water from sewage treatment plant

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: RSD: Relative Standard Deviation. ND: Non-detected.

head.ce:title: Facile preparation of N, S co-doped carbon dots and their application to a novel off-on fluorescent probe for selective determination of Hg2+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Resource Clean Conversion in Ethnic Regions, Education Department of Yunnan, School of Chemistry and Environment, Yunnan Minzu University, Kunming, 650500, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Resource Clean Conversion in Ethnic Regions

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Resource Clean Conversion in Ethnic Regions, Education Department of Yunnan, School of Chemistry and Environment, Yunnan Minzu University, Kunming, 650500, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Chemistry and Biotechnology, School of Life and Environmental Sciences, Deakin University, Waurn Ponds, Victoria, 3216, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life and Environmental Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre for Chemistry and Biotechnology, School of Life and Environmental Sciences, Deakin University, Waurn Ponds, Victoria, 3216, Australia

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In this work, citric acid (CA) and glutathione (GSH) were selected as the raw materials for preparation carbon dote co-doped with N and S (NS-CDs) via microwave heating method. The obtained NS-CDs displayed stable and strong blue luminescence with a maximum emission (λem) wavelength of 442 nm and a maximum excitation (λex) wavelength of 371 nm, and their fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) was 10.9%. The fluorescence of NS-CDs was quenched by silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) through a combination of static quenching effect and inner filter effect (IFE), and could be recovered by Hg2+ even at nanomolar levels ascribing to redox reaction between Hg2+ and AgNPs. Based upon this phenomenon, a novel off-on fluorescent probe (NS-CDs - AgNPs) for determination trace Hg2+ in aqueous solution was constructed using the obtained NS-CDs and AgNPs. Under optimal conditions, NS-CDs - AgNPs exhibited a wide linearity range of 10–90 nM (R
2 = 0.993) for Hg2+and a low LOD of 4.2 nM. NS-CDs - AgNPs was successfully utilized for monitoring Hg2+ in real water samples with the recoveries of 91.4–108.6% (RSD <5.84%), which indicated that the fluorescent probe has enormous application potential to detect trace Hg2+ in real water samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Hg2+, a dangerous water pollutant, has been confirmed to cause irreversible damage to various human organs and central nervous system at low concentrations [1–3]. Hg2+ can be accumulated in the aqueous environment and be enriched in aquatic organisms owing to recalcitrant to degradation. Hg2+ has been listed as international priority pollutant, which may cause a serious and long-term threat on human health through the food chain [4–6]. Therefore, it is urgent to develop fast, sensitive and cost-effective detection methods for trace Hg2+ determination in the aqueous environment. The traditional detection methods of trace Hg2+ include atomic fluorescence spectroscopy (AFS) [7], inductively coupled plasma-mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS) [8], X-ray fluorescence spectroscopy (XRF) [9] and atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [10]. Although these methods possess excellent sensitivity and low detection limit, they require complicated sample pretreatment, time-consuming procedures, costly apparatus and professional technical operation, resulting in long detection cycles and high costs [11]. Nowadays, various emerging approaches such as Electrochemical Detection (ED) [12], Surface-Enhanced Raman Scattering (SERS) [13], colorimetric analysis [14] and fluorescence method [15] have been explored to detect trace Hg2+. Among them, fluorescence probes are one of the indispensable tools for detection trace Hg2+ because of their merits of straightforward operation, short assay time, high sensitivity and low price [16].

Traditional fluorescent molecule probes have the advantages of clear luminescence mechanisms, multiple fluorescence colors and high quantum yields [17], but they still manifest some disadvantages, such as complex synthesis [18], utilization of organic solvents [19], and poor water solubility [20]. Therefore, to effectively detect trace Hg2+ in aqueous solutions, there is a need for developing novel fluorescent nanoprobes through a facile synthesis and constructing reliable analytical strategy.

Under these circumstances, a diversity of fluorescent nanoprobes such as semiconductor quantum dots [21], metallic nanoclusters [22] and carbon dots (CDs) [23] are inspired extensive studies owing to their unique electronic properties, size-tunable emission, good biocompatibility, high quantum efficiency and stable fluorescence [24]. Among them, CDs are deemed to be potential competitors to instead traditional metal-containing quantum dots owing to their high quantum yield (QY), admirable photostability, good water solubility, low toxicity, low cost, favorable biocompatibility and easy preparation [25]. Based on these distinct benefits, CDs have been successfully synthesized by using different synthesis methods and carbon sources, and have been used for determination of Hg2+, Cu2+, Cr3+ and Fe3+ [26–28]. Notably, the fluorescent quantum yield (FLQY) and sensing performance can be improved by doped, especially co-doped heteroatoms such as nitrogen and sulfur in CDs [29]. Hence, developing facile and effective methods to prepare nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs (NS-CDs) remain of interest. Furthermore, some CDs systems based on photo-induced electron transfer (PET), static quenching, dynamic quenching, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE) have been reported for trace targets analysis [30]. As far as we know, the process between CDs and AgNPs has not yet been studied well.

Herein, we successfully synthesized NS-CDs by a straightforward, low-cost, environmental and simple microwave-assisted process utilizing CA and GSH as precursor. The obtained NS-CDs exhibited strong and stable blue luminescence. The fluorescence of NS-CDs was dramatically quenched by AgNPs and gradually recovered by Hg2+. In light of this, a greatly sensitive off-on fluorescent nanoprobe (NS-CDs - AgNPs) was designed for monitoring Hg2+ in aqueous solution. The process of NS-CDs–AgNPs synthesis and Hg2+ detection was demonstrated in Scheme 1 .

Silver nitrate (AgNO3) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Regent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glutathione (C10H17N3O6S) was obtained from Shanghai Titan Scientific Co., Let. (Shanghai, China). Potassium acid phthalate (KHP) and Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were provided by Tianjin Fengchuan Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). citric acid (C6H8O7), Hydrochloric acid (HCl), Sodium borohydride (NaBH4), and Sodium chloride (NaCl) were provided by Adamas Reagent Co, Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were AR grade and ultra-pure water was used throughout the experiment.

Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra (UV–Vis) was obtained using a UV-2100 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectra (FL) was measured using a F-7000 fluorescence spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) was acquired from a Nicolet iS10 FT-IR spectrophotometer (Nicolet, USA). Transmission electron microscope image (TEM) was acquired via a JEM-2100 plus transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). The particle size was measured by a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern, UK). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a Thermo fisher Scientific K-Alpha + spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). The fluorescence lifetime was examined on FLS1000 steady-state/lifetime spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh, UK).

AgNPs were prepared according to the literature [31]. Briefly, 0.73 mL of 34 mmol/L sodium citrate aqueous solution was added to 100 mL conical flask containing 0.422 mL of 58.8 mmol/L silver nitrate aqueous solution and vigorous stirring for 30 min. Afterwards, 0.85 mL of 100 mmol/L NaBH4 solution was added and stirred until the solution turned yellow. After continuously agitating for 20 min, the colloid was aged for 2 days at room temperature. Finally, the synthesized AgNPs solution (0.46 g/L) was reserved in a freezer at 4 °C for farther usage.

NS-CDs were successfully prepared by microwave-assisted method using citric acid and glutathione as key materials. In brief, 1.0 g citric acid and 1.0 g glutathione were poured into 20 mL ultra-pure water and dispersed by ultrasonic treatment for 10 min. The solution was heated in a commercial microwave oven at 540 W for 5 min. Subsequently, the brown solid was dissolved in ultra-pure water (20 mL) and centrifuged (12,000 rpm, 15 min) to eliminate the insoluble and large fragments, and further NS-CDs were attained by a vacuum freezing drying process (−40 °C, 48 h). Ultimately, the 0.01 g/L standard solution of NS-CDs was prepared with ultra-pure water and preserved in refrigerator at 4 °C until use. The FLQYof NS-CDs was calculated using quinine sulfate (FLQY = 54% in 0.1 M H2SO4) as a reference substance [32,33] according to the equation:
φ x
=
φ s
×
(
K x
K s
)
×
(
η x
η s
)
2
Where φ is the fluorescence quantum yield; x and s represent the NS-CDs and quinine sulfate, respectively; K is the slope of absorbance to the integral area of the fluorescence spectra (λex = 350 nm); η denote the refractive index of the solvent (η
x /η s is about 1 in aqueous solutions).

The determination of Hg2+ was carried out at room temperature by the following: 30 μL NS-CDs stock solution (0.01 g/L), 520 μL AgNPs (0.46 g/L), 50 μL Hg2+ (1 μM/L) and 2.40 mL KHP buffer solution (pH = 4.0, 50 mM) were transferred into a centrifuge tube. After cultivating for 8 min, the emission spectra were examined with the excitation wavelength of 371 nm and with both emission and excitation slit widths of 5 nm. For selectivity evaluation, the ions including Cd2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Co3+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Au+, Na+, Cr3+ and Zn2+ with an identical concentration were respectively tested through the procedures as mentioned above. In order to assess anti-interference performance, 50 nM Hg2+ and 250 nM of the aforementioned ions were simultaneously added into this system to monitor whether the presence of other ions interfered with Hg2+ detection.

Three actual samples were gathered and utilized to evaluate the feasibility and reliability of NS-CDs – AgNPs. The aqueous samples were obtained from the water pipeline, sewage treatment plant and Laoyu River (Kunming, China). These water samples were first preprocessed with microporous membrane (d = 0.22 μm). Various concentrations of Hg2+ standard solution (20 nM, 50 nM, 80 nM) were added to water samples and then detected by the as-prepared fluorescent nanoprobe with fluorescence spectra.

TEM and DLS were used for investigating the morphology and the size of AgNPs and NS-CDs. As displayed in Fig. 1 a, the synthesized NS-CDs exhibit monodispersed and homogenous in size with the average particle diameter of 3.63 ± 1.92 nm. Fig. 1b reveals that the prepared AgNPs are spherical and have good dispersibility with the mean diameter of 14.29 ± 1.4 nm, which is consistent with the result of literature [31].

The FT-IR spectrum of NS-CDs was tested and illustrated in Fig. 1c. The band at 3359 cm−1 corresponds to O–H/N–H stretching vibrations. The stretching vibration of C–H provokes a peak at 2938 cm−1. The band at 2566 cm−1 corresponds to S–H stretching vibrations [34]. The characteristic peaks at 1710 cm−1 and 1547 cm−1 are related to CO and CC/CN stretching vibrations, respectively. The apparent absorption band at 1397 cm−1 is assigned to the C–N stretching vibration. The peak at 1209 cm−1 represents C–S and C–O stretching vibrations [32,35].

XPS was used to further verify the NS-CDs surface state. Four dominant peaks around 163 eV, 285 eV, 400 eV and 532 eV are obtained in the survey spectrum (Fig. 1d), which are attributed to N1S, O1S, C1S and S1S, respectively [36], suggesting that NS-CDs are mainly consisted of C, N, O and S with the atomic percentages of 61.49%, 9.26%, 27.42% and 1.83%. The binding energy peaks at 286.13, 284.08 and 282.62 eV in high-resolution spectra of C1S (Fig. 2 a), which are assigned to the CO, C–N and C–C/CC groups, respectively. The N1S spectra (Fig. 2b) indicates that the main group is N–C (3) (397.76 eV), the C–N–C bond (399.23 eV) and N–H (399.79 eV) groups may also exist in NS-CDs. There are two peaks at 531.15 eV and 529.77 eV can be seen from O1S spectra (Fig. 2c), which represent C–O–C/C–OH and CO groups. From high-resolution spectra of S1S (Fig. 2d), the S–H (161.64 eV) and C–S (162.27 eV) groups are confirmed on the surface of NS-CDs. Based on the results of FT-IR and XPS, we can draw a conclusion that nitrogen and sulfur elements are co-doped in NS-CDs, and many hydrophilic groups, such as carboxylic groups, amino groups, hydroxyl and thiol group, exist on the surface of the prepared NS-CDs, which improve the water solubility of NS-CDs [37]. Furthermore, there are thiol groups (-SH) on the surface of NS-CDs, which may benefit for binding AgNPs.

The optical properties of NS-CDs were studied using UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 2e). The broad absorption band at 285 is caused by the π–π* transition of the CO bond, while the strong absorption at 348 nm is attributed to the n-π* transition of the CO bond [38]. The aqueous solution of NS-CDs is colourless under natural light and exhibits a violet fluorescence under UV light (365 nm). The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths are 371 and 442 nm, and the Stokes shift is 71 nm. Moreover, an excitation-dependent emission phenomenon can be observed with the change of the excitation wavelength (Fig. 2f). These results indicate that the fluorescence of NS-CDs may be caused by their surface defects, particle sizes and surface light-emitting sites [39]. The FLQY of NS-CDs is about 10.9% in reference quinine sulfate (Fig. S1).

The prepared NS-CDs exhibit high stability in the pH range of 2–12 (Fig. S2a). The fluorescence strength is constant when the temperature ranges from 10 to 80 °C (Fig. S2b), or the concentration of NaCl changes from 0.2 to 1.0 M (Fig. S2c). The fluorescence intensity shows no significant changes after reserving 7 days (Fig. S2d). All the above features display that the obtained NS-CDs have good stability for sensing.

The fluorescence spectrum of NS-CDs, NS-CDs - Hg2+, NS-CDs - AgNPs and NS-CDs-AgNPs - Hg2+ were measured and displayed in Fig. 3 a. The fluorescent intensity of NS-CDs is unaffected by the Hg2+, but it can be efficiently quenched by AgNPs. What is more, the quenched fluorescence of NS-CDs - AgNPs system can be restored after adding Hg2+. This phenomenon indicates that an off-on fluorescent probe based on NS-CDs and AgNPs can be developed and used to detect Hg2+. In addition, the absorption of AgNPs is between 321 nm and 495 nm, the NS-CDs excitation band and emission band are 281–420 nm and 390–600 nm, respectively (Fig. 3b). The excitation and emission of NS-CDs can be absorbed by AgNPs, which indicates that the inner filter effect (IFE) is the fluorescence quenching mechanism. In order to further verify fluorescence quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetimes of NS-CDs, NS-CDs- AgNPs and NS-CDs-AgNPs - Hg2+ were performed and illustrated in Fig. 3c. The average lifetime of NS-CDs before and after adding AgNPs are 9.4034 ns and 9.5735 ns, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime of NS-CDs is hardly changed whether AgNPs in or not. This suggests that a static quenching process occurred in the system. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching mechanism between NS-CDs and AgNPs is the combined effect of both IFE and static quenching effect. However, the average lifetime of NS-CDs-AgNPs - Hg2+ is 7.9244 ns, but the corresponding emission peak is maintained at 442 nm, which implies that the fluorescence property of NS-CDs may be adjusted by AgNPs and Hg2+ [33].

The UV–vis spectroscopy was used to further evaluate the interactions among NS-CDs, AgNPs and Hg2+. As displayed in Fig. 3d, the characteristic absorption peak of AgNPs at 392 nm is reduced by NS-CDs, and the absorption peak of NS-CDs at 348 nm is disappeared simultaneously, suggesting that a NS-CDs-AgNPs complex is formed, AgNPs complexed with the amino groups and thiol groups of NS-CDs may be the best explanation [40]. In order to demonstrate the binding mode of NS-CDs-AgNPs, FT-IR and XPS analysis were carried out. It can be seen from the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S3) that the characteristic bands of S–H group and CC/CN group at 2566 cm−1 and 1547 cm−1 of NS-CDs are disappeared when NS-CDs are mixed with AgNPs. Additionally, the absorption band of CO group is weakened and shifted from 1710 cm−1–1651 cm−1. Simultaneously, the O–H/N–H stretching peaks around 3600 to 3000 cm−1 become sharper. This result indicates that NS-CDs were adsorbed on the AgNPs surface by chemisorption [41]. From XPS spectra (Fig. S4c. Fig. S4d), Ag–S and Ag–N were confirmed in NS-CDs-AgNPs. This is consistent with the FT-IR results, demonstrating that the NS-CDs are bonded to AgNPs via amino groups and thiol group existing on NS-CDs [42].

Furthermore, the absorption peak of NS-CDs-AgNPs complex at 392 nm is gradually decreased and eventually disappeared with the increasing concentration of Hg2+. This may be caused by a redox reaction between AgNPs and Hg2+. Because the standard potential of Ag+/Ag (0.80 V) is lower than that of Hg2+/Hg (0.85 V), which may lead to spontaneous redox reaction of NS-CDs-AgnNPs + Hg2+→ NS-CDs-Agn-2NPsHg + 2Ag+ [43–45]occur. To further verify the reaction mechanism between NS-CDs-AgNPs complex and Hg2+, we confirmed that there are more Ag+ in the system by XPS (Fig. S3e, Fig. S3f). Significantly, the restored fluorescence of NS-CDs-AgNPs - Hg2+ would be generated by NS-CDs-Agn-2NPsHg, this is why the average lifetime of NS-CDs - AgNPs - Hg2+ is 7.9244 ns, and the corresponding emission peak is maintained at 442 nm.

In order to improve the sensitivity of NS-CDs - AgNPs for Hg2+ detection, several important factors, such as the dosage of NS-CDs and AgNPs, pH and the reaction time, were optimized. The △F (△F = F
1
–F
0 , where F
1 and F
0 represent the fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of Hg2+ respectively) was surveyed. The results are shown in Fig S5. The optimal dosage of N, S-CDs and AgNPs are 0.01 g/L and 0.06 g/L, respectively. The ideal pH value is obtained at 4.0, and the best incubation time is 8 min.

The performance of the NS-CDs – AgNPs for Hg2+ detection was evaluated and demonstrated in Fig. 4 a. The fluorescent intensity of NS-CDs – AgNPs at 442 nm is gradually regained as the dosage of Hg2+ increased. It was obvious that a linear correlation can be acquired between △F value and Hg2+ (10–90 nM), and the regression equation is y = 2.054x-1.6444, R 2 = 0.9929, where y represents the restored fluorescence intensity and x is concentration of Hg2+. On the basis of the formula LOD = 3 N/S and LOQ = 10 N/S (N is the standard deviation of blank data, S is the slope of the calibration line), the detection limit (LOD) and the limit of quantification (LOQ) are calculated to be of 4.2 nM and 10.9 nM, respectively. A comparison between this method and other reported methods was made and shown in Table 1 . The probe is more sensitive than most of other methods. What is more, compared with the reported methods, this method is simpler, more selective and cheaper, which indicated that it may have good possibility to the determination of trace Hg2+ in water solution.

The selectivity is a critical parameter of fluorescent sensor. In order to evaluate the selectivity of NS-CDs – AgNPs toward Hg2+, the effects of different metal ions including Cd2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Co3+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Na+, Cr3+, Zn2+ and Hg2+ were examined under the same conditions. As illustrated in Fig. 4b, the ΔF has no significant increase after adding other metal ions (pink bar). Furthermore, there is negligible impact on Hg2+ detection even when the other metal ions mentioned above and Hg2+ were added into the system at the same time (yellow bar). These results indicate that the probe of NS-CDs – AgNPs exhibits a high selectivity and strong anti-interference for Hg2+ detection.

The practicability and reliability of NS-CDs – AgNPs were investigated by analysis of real water simples spiked with three different concentrations of Hg2+ standards (Table 2 ). The recoveries of Hg2+ is between 91.45% and 108.60%, the relative standard deviations (RSD) ranges from 1.46% to 5.58%. These results illustrate that the NS-CDs – AgNPs is a reliable probe for Hg2+detection in real water samples.

NS-CDs which exhibited strong fluorescence, excellent solubility and good dispersibility were rapidly prepared by a microwave method utilizing CA and GSH as raw material. The fluorescence of NS-CDs could be quenched by AgNPs and recovered after adding Hg2+. The quenching mechanism between NS-CDs and AgNPs has been verified as the combined effect of both IFE and static quenching effect. The fluorescence of NS-CDs -AgNPs was restored by Hg2+ owing to the redox reaction between AgNPs and Hg2+. Based on this phenomenon, a facile off-on fluorescent probe was developed for highly sensitive determination of Hg2+ in aqueous samples. The liner range and LOD is of 10–90 nM and 4.2 nM, respectively. Furthermore, the probe shows good accuracy in practical water samples with the recoveries of 91%–108% and RSD of 1.46%–5.84%, suggesting the considerable potential for evaluating trace Hg2+ in aqueous solution.

Pengyuan Yin: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Guixiang Yao: Validation. Tianru Zou: Validation. Nan A: Supervision. Pei Na: Supervision. Wenrong Yang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition. Hongbin Wang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Wei Tan: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by YMU-DEAKIN International Associated Laboratory on Functional Materials; Science and Technology Department of Yunnan Province (No. 202105AC160055); the Foundation of Education Bureau of Yunnan Province (No. 2020J0327), and the College Student Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Project of Yunnan Minzu university (2021) and Yunnan province (2021).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One pot synthesis of highly fluorescent N doped C-dots and used as fluorescent probe detection for Hg2+ and Ag+ in aqueous solution

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Ren, Q. Zhang, S. Li, S. Fu, F. Chai, C. Wang, F. Qu, One pot synthesis of highly fluorescent N doped C-dots and used as fluorescent probe detection for Hg2+ and Ag+ in aqueous solution, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical. 243 (2017) 244-253.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A fluorescence inner-filter effect based sensing platform for turn-on detection of glutathione in human serum

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Tang, X. You, Q. Fang, X. Li, G. Li, J. Chen, W. Chen, A Fluorescence Inner-Filter Effect Based Sensing Platform for Turn-On Detection of Glutathione in Human Serum, Sensors. 19 (2019) 228.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Progress in metal-organic frameworks facilitated mercury detection and removal

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Shellaiah, K.-W. Sun, Progress in Metal-Organic Frameworks Facilitated Mercury Detection and Removal, Chemosensors. 9 (2021) 101.

Shah, Synthesis and characterization of triazole stabilized silver nanoparticles as colorimetric probe for mercury, Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects. 629 (2021) 127419.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots as nanoprobes for sensitive and selective determination of mercury (II) in surface waters
